1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.

1	\chapter{Rigidity over a base field $k$: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve to a group scheme are constant}
2	\label{chap:RigidityKbar}
3	
4	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-$0$ Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an arbitrary base field $k$} (no algebraic closure assumed; see \S "Iter-127 over-k commitment" below). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
5	
6	\begin{quote}
7	  \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
8	\end{quote}
9	
10	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-$0$ universal-property step of $\thm{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}$ that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of $f \colon C \to A$ when $C$ has genus $0$ and a $k$-rational marked point.
11	
12	The full proof of the keystone is gated on a Mathlib pile (cotangent-triviality of a group scheme + scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ + characteristic-$p$ absolute-Frobenius iteration); see \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} for the inventory. The iter-126 scaffolding lands the named declaration with a single \texttt{sorry} body whose residual is exactly the keystone above; the body is closed iter-128+ as the shared pile lands.
13	
14	\paragraph{Iter-127 over-k commitment.} Prior strategy iterations routed through base-change to the algebraic closure $\bar k$ followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c in STRATEGY.md, $\sim 4$--$8$ iter / $300$--$500$ LOC). The iter-127 over-k analogist (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) verified that all three active pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build directly over an arbitrary base field $k$: piece (i) cotangent triviality uses the intrinsic shear iso $(a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)$ (scheme map over any base); piece (ii) \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} is $k$-agnostic in Mathlib; piece (iii) uses absolute Frobenius $F_X$ (intrinsic to $X$, no perfectness/alg-closure required). The Galois-descent step M2.c and its companion \texttt{geomIrred.exists\_kalg\_pt} (M2.c.aux) are DROPPED, saving 7--13 iter / 500--900 LOC. \textbf{The Lean declaration's signature is already $k$-agnostic} (\texttt{[Field kbar]}, no \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]}); only docstrings, the variable name \texttt{kbar} (a legacy from the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ framing; rename to \texttt{k} is a low-priority cleanup refactor scheduled for iter-128+), and this chapter's prose framing shift to the over-k reading.
15	
16	\section{Statement}
17	
18	\begin{theorem}
19	  \label{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar}
21	  \leanok
22	  Let $\bar k$ be a field (in the use case, an algebraic closure of a base field $k$; the statement is intrinsic to $\bar k$ and does not require an ambient base $k$). Let $C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$ (i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$). Let $A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
23	  \[
24	    f \colon C \longrightarrow A
25	  \]
26	  be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes over $\Spec \bar k$, and let $p \in C(\bar k)$ be a $\bar k$-rational point such that $f(p) = \eta_A$. Then $f$ equals the constant morphism at $\eta_A$:
27	  \[
28	    f \;=\; \bigl(C \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\bigr).
29	  \]
30	
31	  \emph{Encoding note (iter-126).} The Lean signature uses the abstract characterization $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{IsProper}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \genus(C) = 0$ instead of a literal $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ scheme. The two encodings are equivalent over $\bar k$ once the curve has a $\bar k$-rational point (Brauer--Severi triviality + Riemann--Roch on $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$); the abstract form is used because Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} does not ship a packaged $\mathtt{ProjectiveSpace}\,n\,S$ as a $\mathtt{Scheme.Over}\,S$, and the C.2.b–C.2.d proof decomposition below works for any smooth proper geometrically integral genus-0 curve over $\bar k$ without explicit $\mathbb P^1$ API.
32	\end{theorem}
33	
34	\begin{remark}
35	  \label{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}
36	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
37	  The conclusion is vacuously true when $A$ is zero-dimensional: in that case $A = \Spec \bar k$ and every morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ trivially. The substantive content of the theorem is the positive-dimensional case, which decomposes into the steps of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}.
38	\end{remark}
39	
40	\section{Proof decomposition}
41	\label{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}
42	
43	The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} decomposes as follows. We reuse the sub-step labelling of Jacobian.tex \S C.2 so cross-references between the two chapters remain unambiguous.
44	
45	\begin{itemize}
46	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The conclusion $f = c$ follows from \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (i.e.\ \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, after the iter-125 refactor) applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $U.\iota \circ f.\mathrm{left} = U.\iota \circ c.\mathrm{left}$ as scheme morphisms. We will take $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The refactored rigidity lemma's hypotheses are trivially satisfied: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is smooth (hence reduced) and geometrically irreducible over $\bar k$, and $A$ is proper (hence separated) over $\bar k$.
47	
48	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map). Dichotomy:
49	  \begin{itemize}
50	    \item If $g = 0$: $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous (\cref{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}).
51	    \item If $g \geq 1$ and the image is a single point: the image must be $\{\eta_A\}$ (the pointed hypothesis $f(p) = \eta_A$ pins it). Then $f$ is constant on underlying topological points, and the conclusion follows by promoting set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality (sub-step C.2.e of Jacobian.tex, applied via \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}).
52	    \item If $g \geq 1$ and the image is one-dimensional: the image is an irreducible rational curve in $A$. This case is ruled out by the keystone classical input (C.2.d below).
53	  \end{itemize}
54	
55	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Keystone: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- the image of $f$ has dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by Mumford's classical fact stated in the chapter introduction. Two standard proofs are sketched in Jacobian.tex \S C.2.d (the dual-abelian-variety route via $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, and the cotangent-bundle route via triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$). Both routes require Mathlib infrastructure not currently present (see \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}).
56	
57	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promotion of set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality.} Standard reduced-source / separated-target argument, integrated into the application of \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} in C.2.b.
58	\end{itemize}
59	
60	\section{The shared cotangent-vanishing Mathlib pile}
61	\label{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}
62	
63	The C.2.d keystone above (\emph{morphisms from $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to a positive-dimensional abelian variety over $\bar k$ are constant}) and the genus-$0$ identification alternative of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (M2.d-alt of STRATEGY.md, which sidesteps Riemann--Roch by running the cotangent-vanishing argument directly) depend on the same Mathlib pile. STRATEGY.md \S M2.d-alt names this the \emph{shared cotangent-vanishing pile}. The iter-126 mathlib-analogist consult (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile.md}) decomposed the pile into the four pieces below, revised the per-piece LOC budgets upward where the directive had under-scoped, and made the characteristic-$p$ option pick:
64	
65	\begin{itemize}
66	  \item[(i)] \emph{Group-scheme cotangent triviality.} The relative cotangent presheaf $\Omega_{G/k}$ of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $G$ over a base field $k$ (no algebraic-closure hypothesis, per the iter-127 over-k commitment) is free of rank $\dim G$. Per the iter-126 analogist's naming-idiom alignment (Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has \emph{no} \texttt{AbelianVariety} file; the \texttt{GrpObj} namespace per Yang+Merten 2026 is the canonical home), target names are \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_free} and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_rank\_eq\_dim}. A prover-ready three-step sub-lemma decomposition (i.a)\,$\to$\,(i.b)\,$\to$\,(i.c) for the iter-128+ build lane is given in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}. Estimated build: \textbf{800--1500 LOC} (iter-126 analogist's correction over an earlier 200--400 LOC framing; the triviality is the chain Lie-algebra-of-\texttt{GrpObj} + shear-iso globalisation + relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation on top of the project's existing \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}, inheriting the presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge cost from \texttt{analogies/cotangent-presheaf-design.md}).
67	
68	  \item[(ii)] \emph{Scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0$ implies $f$ factors through $\Spec k$.} The companion algebraic fact: if a morphism $f \colon X \to Y$ between smooth proper $k$-schemes has $\mathrm d f \colon f^* \Omega_{Y/k} \to \Omega_{X/k}$ the zero map (e.g.\ because $\Omega_{Y/k}$ is trivial and $H^0(X, \Omega_{X/k}) = 0$), then $f$ factors through the constant morphism. In characteristic $0$ this is the standard differential-vanishing-implies-constancy argument. In characteristic $p > 0$ it requires the Frobenius iteration of piece (iii). Per the analogist's recommendation: the ring-level half aligns with Mathlib's \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.DifferentialRing}, lines 62--70; one Mathlib consumer at \texttt{FieldTheory.Differential.Liouville}); the scheme-level half is a NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL gap and the canonical name is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_diff\_zero} (following the iter-125-refactored \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} idiom). Estimated build: \textbf{250--500 LOC}.
69	
70	  \item[(iii)] \emph{Characteristic-$p$ handling --- \textbf{committed Option A: Frobenius iteration}.} Per the analogist's decisive pick (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile.md} \S "Option A wins"): the three options on the table are
71	  \begin{itemize}
72	    \item Frobenius iteration (replace $f$ with $f \circ F^n$ for the $n$-th iterate of Frobenius; descent of the conclusion uses smoothness of $A$);
73	    \item Mumford's argument (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4: abelian varieties contain no rational curves in characteristic $p$);
74	    \item Lifting to characteristic $0$ via Witt vectors.
75	  \end{itemize}
76	  Options B (Mumford) and C (Witt) each carry standalone multi-thousand-LOC dependencies (Mumford requires the very Pic$^0$ / cotangent machinery the project is trying to streamline; Witt-vector lifting requires a scheme-level Witt functor + smooth deformation + descent that Mathlib entirely lacks). Option A consumes Mathlib's first-class ring-level \texttt{frobenius} / \texttt{iterateFrobenius} from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius} (lines 30--110+) and aligns with the project's existing scheme-level Frobenius design context (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean:48--55}); the scheme-level Frobenius lift remains a NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL gap, but Option A's net cost is dominated by adapting the ring-level idiom upward to schemes. Estimated build: \textbf{300--600 LOC}.
77	
78	  \item[(iv)] \emph{Serre duality on a smooth proper curve --- \textbf{DEFERRED as named gap}.} Used by the M2.d-alt genus-$0$ identification alternative, NOT by C.2.d itself. 114	\leanok

116	  \[

123	  \label{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}
124	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_iso_localRingCotangent}
125	  % Lean signature stub (iter-130+ build target; bridges the iter-128 body
126	  % construction to the local-ring cotangent space supplied by Mathlib):
127	  %   noncomputable def cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_iso_localRingCotangent
128	  %       (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))

130	  %       [IsProper G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] :
131	  %       cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G ≅
132	  %         ModuleCat.of k (Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace
133	  %           (R := Scheme.stalk G.left (η_G.left.base ⟨_, trivial⟩)))
134	  \uses{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}

Then there is a canonical $k$-linear isomorphism
136	  \[
137	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;=\; \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}
138	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
139	    \mathfrak m_{\eta_G} \big/ \mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}.
140	  \]
141	  Concretely, the right-hand side is \texttt{Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace} applied to the local ring $\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$ (as a module over its residue field, identified with $k$ via the $\GrpObj$-supplied identity section); the left-hand side is the iter-128 evaluate-then-extend-scalars Lean body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}.

143	
144	\begin{proof}
145	  \uses{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
146	  The bridge is the composition of two standard identifications (both at the level of $k$-modules):
147	
148	  \emph{Step 1: from global differentials to the differential module of the local stalk-residue map.} The iter-128 body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} extends scalars of $(\Omega_{G/k})(G)$ along the ring map $\psi \colon \Gamma(G, \mathcal O_{G}) \to k$ induced by $\eta_G$ on global sections. Factor $\psi$ through the localisation at the identity stalk and the residue map,
149	  \[
150	    \Gamma(G, \mathcal O_{G}) \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{loc}\;}\; \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G} \;\xrightarrow{\;\pi\;}\; k.
151	  \]
152	  Localisation kills relative differentials (\texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton\_kaehlerDifferential}, used already as \cref{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton} of Differentials.tex), so extension of scalars along the localisation step preserves $\Omega$ as the Kähler differential module $\Omega_{\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}/k}$. The full extension along $\psi$ is therefore $k \otimes_{\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}} \Omega_{\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}/k}$.
153	
154	  \emph{Step 2: from the Kähler module of a local $k$-algebra with residue field $k$ to $\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}$.} For a local ring $(R, \mathfrak m, k)$ that is a $k$-algebra with residue map $\pi \colon R \to k$ a $k$-algebra retraction (here supplied by the identity section $\eta_G$ producing $R = \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$ over $k$ with residue field canonically $k$), the standard identification
155	  \[
156	    k \,\otimes_{R}\, \Omega_{R/k} \;\;\cong\;\; \mathfrak m \big/ \mathfrak m^{2}
157	  \]
158	  holds via the universal derivation $\mathrm{d} \colon R \to \Omega_{R/k}$ restricted to $\mathfrak m$ (the kernel of $\pi$). This is Stacks Tag~02G1 (or Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~II.8.7 in the Noetherian case) and is in Mathlib at the ring level via \texttt{Algebra.Extension.CotangentSpace} infrastructure (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Extension.Cotangent.Basic}); the present blueprint statement chooses the local-ring framing \texttt{Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Cotangent}, the abbreviation \texttt{(maximalIdeal R).Cotangent}) as the cleanest container for the rank step in \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}.

160	  Composing Steps 1 and 2 yields the displayed isomorphism. No regularity input is needed for the bridge itself: the bridge is a tautological identification of two constructions of the same $k$-module, valid for any group scheme with an identity section in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$. Regularity enters only at the rank-pinning step (\cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}).
161	\end{proof}

184	\begin{proof}
185	  \uses{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace, lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}
186	  Combine four standard pieces.
187	
188	  \emph{Step 1: bridge to the local-ring cotangent space.} By \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}, the iter-128 evaluate-then-extend-scalars body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} is $k$-linearly isomorphic to $\mathfrak m_{\eta_G} / \mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}$, the local-ring cotangent space of $\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$. 189	
190	  \emph{Step 2: regularity of the local ring at the identity.} Smoothness of $f \colon G \to \Spec k$ at the closed point $\eta_G$ implies that $\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$ is a regular local ring (\texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}; Stacks Tag~00TR / Hartshorne~II.10.3.B). The Krull dimension of $\mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$ equals the relative dimension $n$ of $f$ at $\eta_G$: this is the standard smooth-implies-rel-dim-equals-Krull-dim-of-the-stalk fact (Stacks Tag~02G1 / Hartshorne~II.10.4 for the smooth case). Both facts package together in the Mathlib \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~$n$~$f$ class via the local-ring instance attached to a closed point of a smooth scheme.
191	
192	  \emph{Step 3: cotangent rank equals Krull dimension for a regular local ring.} For a regular local ring $(R, \mathfrak m)$, the cotangent space \texttt{Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace}~$R$ is finitely generated free over the residue field with rank equal to the Krull dimension; this is essentially the defining property of regularity (and is precisely the equivalence between Noetherian-local minimal-number-of-generators and Krull-dim that \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} packages). Combined with Step 2: $\finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2} = \dim \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G} = n$.
193	
194	  \emph{Step 4: cross-check via the affine-chart Kähler rank.} An alternative route through the project's already-formalised forward direction of the smoothness criterion (\cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}, i.e.\ \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}) reaches the same rank: on a standard-smooth affine chart $(U, V)$ around $\eta_G$, the algebra-Kähler module $\Omega_{B/A}$ is free of rank $n$ by \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}); extension of scalars along the residue map at $\eta_G$ preserves the rank to give $\finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak g^{\vee} = n$. 196	  Finally, $\finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak g = \finrank_{k}\,\mathrm{Hom}_{k}(\mathfrak g^{\vee}, k) = \finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak g^{\vee} = n$ by the standard rank-equals-rank-of-dual identity for finitely-generated free modules over a field.

201	\begin{lemma}[(i.b) Shear-iso globalisation of the cotangent]
202	  \label{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.mulRight_globalises_cotangent}
204	  \uses{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
205	  \notready
206	  Let $\mu \colon G \times_{k} G \to G$ denote the multiplication of $G$ and let $\sigma \colon G \times_{k} G \to G \times_{k} G$ be the \emph{shear isomorphism}
207	  \[
208	    \sigma \;=\; \langle \pr_{1},\, \mu \rangle, \qquad (a, b) \;\mapsto\; (a,\, a \cdot b),
209	  \]
210	  built categorically from $\mu$ and the binary-product projections in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$. Then $\sigma$ is an isomorphism over the first projection, and pullback along $\sigma$ together with the canonical first-projection-cotangent identification $\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \cong \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}$ yields a natural isomorphism
211	  \[
212	    \Omega_{G/k} \;\cong\; \pr_{1}^{*}\!\bigl(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\bigr) \qquad \text{on } G,
213	  \]
214	  i.e.\ the relative cotangent of $G$ is canonically isomorphic to the constant $\mathcal O_{G}$-module with fibre $\mathfrak g^{\vee}$ trivialised along the structure map $G \to \Spec k$.
215	
216	  \paragraph{Iter-127 over-k risk register.} The globalisation \textbf{must} be formulated functorially via the shear scheme map $\sigma$ — which is a morphism in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$, valid for any base field — and \textbf{not} as a pointwise translation by closed (or $\bar k$-rational) points of $G$. The pointwise-translation argument requires either alg-closure of $k$ or a Nullstellensatz-style $\bar k$-point lifting (as in Mathlib's \texttt{pointEquivClosedPoint}, used in the alg-closed-base branch of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth}); the functorial shear formulation sidesteps that obstacle entirely.
217	\end{lemma}

219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
221	  Build $\sigma = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle$ in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$ from the $\GrpObj$ data only. The candidate inverse
222	  \[
223	    \tau \;=\; \langle \pr_{1},\, \mu \circ (\iota \times \mathrm{id}_{G}) \rangle, \qquad (a, b) \;\mapsto\; (a,\, a^{-1} \cdot b),
224	  \]
225	  where $\iota$ is the inverse on $G$, is again a scheme map in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$. One verifies $\sigma \circ \tau = \mathrm{id}_{G \times_{k} G} = \tau \circ \sigma$ using the $\GrpObj$ axioms (associativity and left-inverse). With $\sigma$ an isomorphism, pullback identifies the relative cotangent for the first projection. Restriction along the section $\langle \mathrm{id}_{G},\, \eta_{G} \rangle \colon G \to G \times_{k} G$ evaluates the second-coordinate cotangent at $\eta_{G}$, yielding the displayed isomorphism. The construction relies only on $\GrpObj$ scheme maps and the existing $\GrpObj.\mathtt{mulRight}$ infrastructure of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth} (\texttt{b80f227}, lines 50--51); no point-set translation enters.

When $C(k) = \emptyset$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the field is \textbf{vacuously true} at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\ldots$ is trivially satisfied when the type $\mathbf{1} \to C$ is empty.
270	  \item \textbf{No other in-tree consumer.} The declaration is project-internal; it is not consumed by any \texttt{Cohomology/*.lean}, \texttt{Genus.lean}, \texttt{AbelJacobi.lean}, or \texttt{Differentials.lean} declaration.
271	\end{itemize}

181	## Presheaf-vs-sheaf coincidence theorem (the absence)
182	
183	For posterity: **there is no Mathlib coincidence theorem** stating that `(relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom).obj (op ⊤)` agrees with `H^0(G, Ω_{G/k}_{sheaf})` for proper geometrically integral G. Such a theorem is in fact **false in general** for the iter-128 body: the presheaf-side is zero (as computed in Decision 1), while the sheaf-side has rank `g · dim G` (Hodge theory). The presheaf-side construction is the **wrong functor for the global-sections computation on non-affine schemes**, which is the geometric content the strategy-critic flagged.

\begin{proof}

\begin{proof}

\leanok
  The identity section $\eta_G$ is a canonical $k$-rational point of $G$ supplied by the $\GrpObj$ structure. The iter-130 Lean construction realises $\mathfrak g^{\vee} = \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}$ as a chart-base-changed Kähler module: by the forward Jacobian criterion \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} (already in the tree as \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}), there exists an affine chart $V \subseteq G$ around the image of the identity section $\eta_G(\mathrm{pt})$, with affine base chart $U \subseteq \Spec k$ and the structural ring map $\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(G, V)$ exhibiting \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} $n$ (and consequently $\Omega_{\Gamma(G,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}$ is a free $\Gamma(G, V)$-module of rank $n$ by \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}). The cotangent space at the identity is then defined as the base change to $k$ of this algebraic Kähler module along the ring map $\psi_V \colon \Gamma(G, V) \to k$ obtained by restricting the identity section $\eta_G$ to $V$ and composing with the canonical isomorphism $\Gamma(\Spec k, \cdot) \cong k$:
  \[
    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;\;:=\;\; k \,\otimes_{\Gamma(G,\,V)}\, \Omega_{\Gamma(G,\,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}.
  \]
  This is a finitely generated free $k$-module of rank $n$ by base-change of a free module along $\psi_V$ (the rank is preserved; see \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}).

  \emph{Caveat on canonicity.} The construction depends on a chart choice $V$ extracted via \texttt{Classical.choice} from the existential statement of \texttt{smooth\_locally\_free\_omega}; it is therefore not canonically attached to $G$ in the strictest sense. For the live consumer (rigidity over $k$, via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), the existence of a rank-$n$ free $k$-module is the only structural content needed, so canonicity is not load-bearing. A canonical (chart-independent) presentation via the stalk-side cotangent $\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}$ is available as a future bridge (\cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} below, currently \texttt{\textbackslash notready}) at additional cost; the project defers that bridge until a non-rigidity consumer materialises that requires canonicity.
\end{proof}

152	  \[
153	    \Gamma(G, \mathcal O_{G}) \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{loc}\;}\; \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G} \;\xrightarrow{\;\pi\;}\; k.
154	  \]

156	
157	  \emph{Step 2: from the Kähler module of a local $k$-algebra with residue field $k$ to $\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}$.} For a local ring $(R, \mathfrak m, k)$ that is a $k$-algebra with residue map $\pi \colon R \to k$ a $k$-algebra retraction (here supplied by the identity section $\eta_G$ producing $R = \mathcal O_{G, \eta_G}$ over $k$ with residue field canonically $k$), the standard identification
158	  \[
159	    k \,\otimes_{R}\, \Omega_{R/k} \;\;\cong\;\; \mathfrak m \big/ \mathfrak m^{2}

207	  \label{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises}
208	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.mulRight_globalises_cotangent}

210	  \notready
211	  Let $\mu \colon G \times_{k} G \to G$ denote the multiplication of $G$ and let $\sigma \colon G \times_{k} G \to G \times_{k} G$ be the \emph{shear isomorphism}
212	  \[
213	    \sigma \;=\; \langle \pr_{1},\, \mu \rangle, \qquad (a, b) \;\mapsto\; (a,\, a \cdot b),
214	  \]
215	  built categorically from $\mu$ and the binary-product projections in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$. Then $\sigma$ is an isomorphism over the first projection, and pullback along $\sigma$ together with the canonical first-projection-cotangent identification $\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \cong \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}$ yields a natural isomorphism
216	  \[
217	    \Omega_{G/k} \;\cong\; \pr_{1}^{*}\!\bigl(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\bigr) \qquad \text{on } G,
218	  \]
219	  i.e.\ the relative cotangent of $G$ is canonically isomorphic to the constant $\mathcal O_{G}$-module with fibre $\mathfrak g^{\vee}$ trivialised along the structure map $G \to \Spec k$.
220	
221	  \paragraph{Iter-127 over-k risk register.} The globalisation \textbf{must} be formulated functorially via the shear scheme map $\sigma$ — which is a morphism in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$, valid for any base field — and \textbf{not} as a pointwise translation by closed (or $\bar k$-rational) points of $G$. 229	  \]

With $\sigma$ an isomorphism, pullback identifies the relative cotangent for the first projection.
